goal: Compare camera sun-glare vs LIDAR returns at 07:00 outdoor loading yard; honest trade-off with sim-gap caveat.
thoughts: Sim sun-glare in Isaac Sim is a LENS-FLARE artifact, not a real CMOS saturation model — state this. LIDAR (905 nm and 1550 nm) unaffected by visible sunlight except for retro-reflectors; Isaac Sim does NOT differentiate wavelengths in the RTX lidar model. Compute: camera 1080p@30 ~ 60 MB/s uncompressed; 32-beam lidar ~ 655k pts/s. Answer 'not unconditionally better.'
code:
# 1. Snapshot the glare case
inspect_camera(prim_path="/World/amr/front_cam")
capture_camera_image(
    camera_prim="/World/amr/front_cam",
    output_path="<GLARE_PNG>",
    output_type="rgb"
)

# 2. LIDAR point density in the same field
run_stage_analysis()
# Inspect /World/amr/.../lidar return count in the front sector.

# 3. Sensor-choice tradeoff inputs
lookup_product_spec(product="<905nm_vs_1550nm_LIDAR>")

# Honest reporting:
# - Sim glare is lens-flare overlay, not CMOS saturation. sim-to-real gap.
# - Isaac Sim RTX LIDAR does not model 905 vs 1550 nm differently.
# - Camera 1080p@30: ~60 MB/s raw; LIDAR 32-beam: ~655k pts/s, much lighter.
# - LIDAR sees through glare; camera degrades — but LIDAR is sparse and misses texture/color classes.
# - Verdict: retain fusion; do not drop camera solely on a sim artifact.
failure_modes: Claiming sim perfectly models CMOS saturation / bloom
Claiming sim differentiates 905 vs 1550 nm LIDAR behavior
Declaring LIDAR unconditionally superior — ignores color/texture loss
Quoting sim FPS as proof — real driver has different compute budget
